1: # CLAUDE.md
2: 
3: This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
4: 
5: ## Project Overview
6: 
7: Awesome Paper Reviewer is a Claude Code skill-based system that acts as a conference reviewer for academic papers. Given a conference name, it produces review opinions in that conference's required format. It deeply understands paper principles, formulas, and conclusions before reviewing. Users can rebut, ask questions, re-read papers, and iteratively update reviews.
8: 
9: **Division of labor**: Claude Code handles all text understanding, reasoning, and review generation. Gemini handles only multimodal analysis (figures, diagrams, charts) via `tools/gemini_vision.py`.
10: 
11: ## Ground Truth
12: 
13: `RESEARCH.md` in the project root is the ground truth for project state.
14: 
15: - Read it at session start.
16: - Prefer asking the user over guessing when state is ambiguous.
17: 
18: ## Session Recovery
19: 
20: On new session or after context compaction:
21: 1. Read `RESEARCH.md` **Pipeline Status** section first (30-second orient).
22: 2. Resume from **Active TODO** — do not restart from scratch.
23: 
24: ## CRITICAL: PDF Policy
25: 
26: **Remote PDFs are banned.** Never download, link to, or suggest PDF files from the internet. All remote paper reading uses LaTeX source only.
27: 
28: **Local PDFs are allowed** via `/read-pdf` (docling extraction). When a user provides a local PDF file path, use the `read-pdf` skill to parse it. LaTeX source from arXiv remains the preferred format — always suggest `/read-paper` when an arXiv ID is detected.
29: 
30: - Download LaTeX source from `https://arxiv.org/e-print/ID` (not `/pdf/ID`)
31: - Source is extracted to `papers/inbox/{arxiv_id}/*.tex` and read with Read/Grep tools
32: - If a paper has no LaTeX source (scanned/older), suggest `/read-pdf` for local PDFs or read the abstract/HTML version instead
33: - Remote PDF download is never allowed — no exceptions
34: 
35: ## Gemini Integration
36: 
37: Figure and image analysis uses the Gemini API via `tools/gemini_vision.py`:
38: 
39: - **SDK**: `google-genai` (new unified SDK, NOT the deprecated `google-generativeai`)
40: - **API Key**: Stored in `.env` as `GEMINI_API_KEY`, loaded via `python-dotenv`
41: - **Models**: `gemini-2.5-flash` (default, fast), `gemini-2.5-pro` (optional, higher quality)
42: - **Usage patterns**:
43:   - PDF papers: Upload entire PDF to Gemini File API for figure analysis (`pdf-figures` subcommand)
44:   - LaTeX papers: Send individual `\includegraphics` images to Gemini (`analyze` subcommand)
45: - **Rule**: Text understanding stays with Claude Code. Gemini is ONLY used for multimodal analysis (figures, diagrams, charts).
46: 
47: ## Tools
48: 
49: Python scripts in `tools/` provide API adapters for the skills:
50: 
51: | Script | Purpose | Invocation |
52: |--------|---------|------------|
53: | `tools/arxiv_fetch.py` | arXiv search + LaTeX source download (stdlib only) | `uv run python tools/arxiv_fetch.py {search\|download\|paper} ...` |
54: | `tools/pdf_parse.py` | PDF parsing via docling (local files only) | `uv run python tools/pdf_parse.py parse <path> [options]` |
55: | `tools/gemini_vision.py` | Figure/image analysis via Gemini API (multimodal) | `uv run python tools/gemini_vision.py {analyze\|pdf-figures} ...` |
56: 
57: ## Paper Library
58: 
59: Local LaTeX source uses a **dual-zone** structure:
60: 
61: ```
62: papers/
63: ├── inbox/                # Newly read papers (not yet reviewed)
64: │   └── {arxiv_id}/       # Flat — no domain classification yet
65: ├── archive/              # Reviewed papers, organized by domain
66: │   ├── safety_alignment/
67: │   ├── training_scaling/
68: │   ├── reasoning/
69: │   ├── architecture/
70: │   ├── efficiency/
71: │   ├── multimodal/
72: │   ├── retrieval_rag/
73: │   ├── evaluation/
74: │   ├── data_synthesis/
75: │   └── agent/
76: ```
77: 
78: **Lifecycle**: `/read-paper` downloads to `papers/inbox/{id}/` → review moves to `papers/archive/{domain}/{id}/`
79: - `/read-pdf` parses local PDFs to `papers/inbox/{slug}/` (contains `paper.md`, `metadata.json` instead of `.tex` files)
80: - `inbox/` = "not yet reviewed"; `archive/` = "reviewed and classified"
81: 
82: ## Knowledge Base
83: 
84: ```
85: knowledge/
86: ├── inbox/                    # Notes for newly read papers
87: │   └── summary_{tag}.md
88: ├── archive/                  # Notes for reviewed papers
89: │   └── summary_{tag}.md
90: ├── reviews/                  # Paper review outputs
91: │   └── review_{tag}.md
92: └── syntheses/                # Cross-paper analysis
93: ```
94: 
95: - Each note covers: Problem, Method, Key Equations, Figures, Experiments, Key Insights, Connections, Questions
96: - The `Connections` section in each note is critical for cross-paper analysis
97: - `/read-paper` and `/read-pdf` write to `knowledge/inbox/`
98: - Reviews are written to `knowledge/reviews/review_{tag}.md`
99: 
100: ## Skills
101: 
102: Skills in `.claude/skills/` are invoked by name:
103: 
104: ### Paper Reading
105: - `read-paper` — deep-read an arXiv paper from LaTeX source + Gemini figure analysis: download .tex, locate entrypoint, recursively read all sections, analyze figures with Gemini, produce structured notes saved to `knowledge/inbox/summary_{tag}.md`
106: - `read-pdf` — deep-read a local academic PDF using docling + Gemini figure analysis: parse PDF to Markdown, analyze figures via Gemini File API, produce structured notes. Use when LaTeX source is unavailable.
107: - `arxiv` — search arXiv API by keyword, download LaTeX source, present structured summaries
108: 
109: ### Review
110: - `review` — Generate a conference-format review: takes paper tag + conference name, deeply re-reads the paper with Gemini figure analysis, produces structured review following the conference's official format. Saved to `knowledge/reviews/review_{tag}.md`.
111: 
112: ### Rebuttal
113: - `rebuttal` — Handle author rebuttals, re-read requests, and clarifying questions: re-reads relevant sections, evaluates rebuttal validity against paper content, updates the review with `[Updated after rebuttal]` markers, adjusts rating if warranted.
114: 
115: ## Conference Review Templates
116: 
117: Templates in `templates/conferences/` define official review formats:
118: 
119: | File | Conference | Overall Rating | Confidence | Extra Scoring Dimensions |
120: |------|-----------|----------------|------------|--------------------------|
121: | `neurips.md` | NeurIPS | 1-10 | 1-5 | — |
122: | `icml.md` | ICML | 1-10 | 1-5 | — |
123: | `iclr.md` | ICLR | 1-10 | 1-5 | — |
124: | `acl.md` | ACL (ARR) | 1-5 (half-point) | 1-5 | Soundness 1-5, Excitement 1-5, Reproducibility 1-5, Datasets 1-5, Software 1-5 |
125: | `cvpr.md` | CVPR | 1-5 | 1-5 | — |
126: 
127: **Important**: The review skill must follow each conference template's Required Sections and Format Template exactly. Different conferences have different sections and scoring scales.
128: 
129: ## Review Workflow
130: 
131: The review process follows this pipeline:
132: 
133: 1. **Read** — Use `/read-paper` or `/read-pdf` to deeply understand the paper (principles, formulas, conclusions) with Gemini figure analysis
134: 2. **Review** — `/review tag Conference` generates a conference-format review with deep citations
135: 3. **Rebuttal** — `/rebuttal tag "text"` evaluates rebuttals, re-reads relevant sections, updates the review
136: 4. **Iterate** — Multiple rebuttal rounds supported; each adds `[Updated after rebuttal]` markers
137: 
138: ### Review Depth Requirements
139: 
140: Reviews must demonstrate deep understanding:
141: - Cite specific equations by number and explain their implications
142: - Reference specific experimental results (tables, figures) with numbers
143: - Identify assumptions in proofs/theorems and whether they hold
144: - Check consistency between claims and evidence
145: - Assess novelty relative to prior work cited in the paper
146: 
147: ### Rebuttal Handling
148: 
149: When a user submits a rebuttal:
150: 1. Re-read the relevant paper sections that address the rebuttal points
151: 2. Evaluate the rebuttal's validity against the paper's actual content (VALID / PARTIALLY VALID / INVALID)
152: 3. Update the review: acknowledge valid points, maintain or adjust critiques, revise scores if warranted
153: 4. Clearly mark updated sections with `[Updated after rebuttal]`
154: 
155: ## Invocation Graph
156: 
157: - Reading pipeline: `arxiv` (search) → `read-paper` (deep read + Gemini figures) → review
158: - PDF reading: `read-pdf` (docling + Gemini figures) → review
159: - Review loop: `review` (conference format + Gemini figures) → `rebuttal` → re-read → update review (iterative)
160: - `read-paper` suggests `read-pdf` as fallback when LaTeX source is unavailable
161: 
162: ## Development Commands
163: 
164: ```bash
165: # Run tools
166: uv run python tools/arxiv_fetch.py search "keyword"
167: uv run python tools/arxiv_fetch.py download 2401.12345
168: uv run python tools/arxiv_fetch.py paper 2401.12345
169: uv run python tools/pdf_parse.py parse /path/to/paper.pdf
170: uv run python tools/gemini_vision.py analyze image.png --prompt "Describe this figure"
171: uv run python tools/gemini_vision.py pdf-figures /path/to/paper.pdf
172: 
173: # Install/sync dependencies
174: uv sync
175: ```
176: 
177: ## Templates
178: 
179: Templates in `templates/`:
180: 
181: - `RESEARCH.md.template` → `RESEARCH.md` (living doc)
182: - `conferences/` — conference review format templates (neurips, icml, iclr, acl, cvpr)

(End of file - total 182 lines)
</content>

1: ---
2: name: review
3: description: Generate a conference-format review for a previously read paper. Takes a paper tag and conference name, deeply re-reads the paper with Gemini figure analysis, and produces a structured review following the conference's official format. Saved to knowledge/reviews/review_{tag}.md.
4: argument-hint: [tag] [conference]
5: allowed-tools: Bash(*), Read, Grep, Glob, Write
6: ---
7: 
8: # Review — Conference-Format Paper Review
9: 
10: Review: $ARGUMENTS
11: 
12: > Reviews must demonstrate **deep understanding** of the paper's principles, formulas, and conclusions.
13: > Weaknesses must cite specific equations, theorems, or experimental results — not vague impressions.
14: 
15: ## Constants
16: 
17: - **KNOWLEDGE_INBOX** — `knowledge/inbox/` — structured notes from reading
18: - **KNOWLEDGE_REVIEWS** — `knowledge/reviews/` — review outputs
19: - **PAPERS_INBOX** — `papers/inbox/` — original paper source files
20: - **CONFERENCE_TEMPLATES** — `templates/conferences/` — conference review format templates
21: - **GEMINI_SCRIPT** — `tools/gemini_vision.py` relative to project root
22: 
23: > Overrides (append to arguments):
24: > - `/review tag NeurIPS - model: gemini-2.5-pro` — use higher-quality Gemini model
25: > - `/review tag NeurIPS - focus: method` — emphasize methodological critique
26: > - `/review tag NeurIPS - focus: experiments` — emphasize experimental rigor
27: 
28: ## Workflow
29: 
30: ### Step 1: Parse Arguments
31: 
32: Parse `$ARGUMENTS` for:
33: 
34: - **Tag**: the paper's tag (used to find notes and source files)
35: - **Conference**: conference name (case-insensitive, partial match supported)
36: - **`- model: MODEL`**: Gemini model override (default: `gemini-2.5-flash`)
37: - **`- focus: AREA`**: review emphasis — `method` / `experiments` (default: balanced)
38: 
39: **Conference name normalization**:
40: 
41: | Input | Normalized |
42: |-------|-----------|
43: | neurips, NeurIPS, NIPS | neurips |
44: | icml, ICML | icml |
45: | iclr, ICLR | iclr |
46: | acl, ACL | acl |
47: | cvpr, CVPR | cvpr |
48: 
49: ### Step 2: Locate Paper Notes
50: 
51: Find the structured notes for this paper:
52: 
53: 1. Check `knowledge/inbox/summary_{tag}.md`
54: 2. If not found, check `knowledge/archive/summary_{tag}.md`
55: 3. If not found, list available summaries and ask the user to read the paper first:
56: 
57: ```text
58: No notes found for tag "{tag}". Please read the paper first:
59:   /read-paper ARXIV_ID    — for arXiv papers (LaTeX source)
60:   /read-pdf /path/to.pdf  — for local PDFs
61: ```
62: 
63: Read the notes file completely. Extract key information: title, authors, source path, problem, method, equations, experiments, insights.
64: 
65: ### Step 3: Locate Original Paper Source
66: 
67: Determine the source type from the notes:
68: 
69: - If notes contain `**arXiv**: [...]` → LaTeX source in `papers/inbox/{arxiv_id}/`
70: - If notes contain `**Source**: PDF (via docling)` → PDF-derived Markdown in `papers/inbox/{slug}/`
71: - If notes contain `**PDF path**: ...` → original PDF still available at that path
72: 
73: Verify the source files exist. If the original source is missing, proceed with notes only but note the limitation.
74: 
75: ### Step 4: Load Conference Template
76: 
77: Read the appropriate conference template:
78: 
79: ```bash
80: Read templates/conferences/{normalized_name}.md
81: ```
82: 
83: If the conference is not in the supported list (`neurips`, `icml`, `iclr`, `acl`, `cvpr`):
84: 
85: 1. List available conferences
86: 2. Ask the user to choose one, or proceed with a generic review format
87: 
88: The template defines: required sections, scoring rubric, confidence scale, format example.
89: 
90: ### Step 5: Deep Re-read for Review
91: 
92: The initial reading notes are not sufficient for a thorough review. Re-read the original source for depth:
93: 
94: **For LaTeX papers**:
95: 1. Re-read the Method section — verify equation derivations, check assumptions in theorems
96: 2. Re-read the Experiments section — check for missing baselines, verify claimed improvements
97: 3. Re-read the Appendix (if available) — check proofs, additional experiments
98: 4. Focus on identifying: gaps between claims and evidence, unstated assumptions, alternative explanations
99: 
100: **For PDF papers**:
101: 1. Re-read `papers/inbox/{slug}/paper.md` — focus on Method and Experiments sections
102: 2. Cross-reference numbers in tables with claims in the text
103: 3. Check if equations are internally consistent
104: 
105: **Review-specific reading priorities**:
106: - Identify every theorem/proposition and its assumptions
107: - Check if experimental comparisons are fair (same hyperparameters, compute, data)
108: - Note if key baselines are missing
109: - Identify limitations the authors don't mention
110: - Check reproducibility: are hyperparameters, code, datasets specified?
111: 
112: ### Step 6: Gemini Figure Analysis
113: 
114: Figures often contain critical information not captured in text (architecture diagrams, result plots, ablation studies). Re-analyze with Gemini for review depth.
115: 
116: **For LaTeX papers**:
117: ```bash
118: grep -rn '\\includegraphics' papers/inbox/{arxiv_id}/
119: uv run python tools/gemini_vision.py analyze "IMAGE_PATHS..." --prompt "For review purposes, critically analyze this figure: What does it show? Are the axes labeled? Is the comparison fair? Any misleading aspects?"
120: ```
121: 
122: **For PDF papers**:
123: ```bash
124: uv run python tools/gemini_vision.py pdf-figures "ORIGINAL_PDF_PATH"
125: ```
126: 
127: Focus Gemini prompts on **critical analysis**, not just description:
128: - "Are the comparisons in this figure fair and complete?"
129: - "Does this architecture diagram match the method description?"
130: - "Are error bars or confidence intervals shown?"
131: 
132: ### Step 7: Generate Review
133: 
134: Based on the conference template, notes, deep re-reading, and Gemini figure analysis, generate the review.
135: 
136: **CRITICAL: Follow the conference template exactly.** Output ALL sections listed in the template's "Required Sections" and "Format Template" — in the order specified by the template. Different conferences have different sections and scoring scales. Do NOT hardcode a fixed section list; instead, read and replicate the structure from `templates/conferences/{conference}.md`.
137: 
138: For example:
139: - **ACL** requires: Paper Summary, Summary of Strengths, Summary of Weaknesses, Questions, Soundness (1–5), Excitement (1–5), Overall Assessment (1–5), Best Paper Justification, Reviewer Confidence (1–5), Limitations and Societal Impact, Ethical Concerns, Needs Ethics Review, Reproducibility (1–5), Datasets (1–5), Software (1–5), Comments/Suggestions/Typos
140: - **NeurIPS** requires: Summary, Strengths, Weaknesses, Questions, Limitations, Ethics Concerns, Reproducibility, Rating (1–10), Confidence (1–5), Constructive Suggestions
141: - **ICML** requires: Summary, Strengths, Weaknesses, Questions, Impact, Reproducibility, Rating (1–10), Confidence (1–5), Constructive Suggestions
142: - **ICLR** requires: Summary, Strengths, Weaknesses, Questions, Reproducibility, Resources, Rating (1–10), Confidence (1–5), Constructive Suggestions
143: - **CVPR** requires: Summary, Strengths, Weaknesses, Questions, Significance, Presentation Quality, Rating (1–5), Confidence (1–5), Constructive Suggestions
144: 
145: **Quality requirements for common sections** (these apply regardless of conference):
146: 
147: 1. **Summary** (3-5 sentences): Demonstrate deep understanding — not just restating the abstract. Show you understand the core technical contribution.
148: 
149: 2. **Strengths** (at least 3): Each strength must cite specific evidence:
150:    - "Equation 3 provides a principled way to decompose the loss into interpretable components"
151:    - "Table 2 shows consistent improvement across all 5 benchmarks (2-5% absolute gain)"
152:    - "The proof of Theorem 2 elegantly establishes convergence under weaker assumptions than prior work"
153: 
154: 3. **Weaknesses** (at least 3): Each weakness must cite specific evidence:
155:    - "Equation 5 assumes i.i.d. data (stated in Appendix A.2), but the experiments in Section 5 use sequential data, creating a mismatch between theory and practice"
156:    - "Table 3 compares only against 2 out of the 5 baselines from [Reference]. The missing baselines (Method X, Method Y) are directly comparable and reported superior in their respective papers"
157:    - "Figure 4's y-axis starts at 0.85 instead of 0, making the 2% improvement appear larger than it is"
158: 
159: 4. **Questions** (at least 3): Show deep engagement:
160:    - "How does the method perform when the assumption in Equation 2 is violated?"
161:    - "The ablation in Table 4 removes component A and B separately — what about removing both?"
162:    - "Is the computational overhead (reported in Appendix C) acceptable for real-time applications?"
163: 
164: 5. **Scoring**: Use the exact scale and increments defined in the conference template (e.g., ACL uses half-point increments 1–5, NeurIPS uses integers 1–10). Include a **justification paragraph** for every scored dimension.
165: 
166: 6. **Suggestions**: Actionable improvements, not vague advice:
167:    - "Add comparison with [Method X] on the same benchmarks using the authors' official implementation"
168:    - "Include a theoretical analysis for the non-i.i.d. setting, or explicitly state it as a limitation"
169: 
170: ### Step 8: Save Review
171: 
172: Write the review to `knowledge/reviews/review_{tag}.md`.
173: 
174: If a review already exists:
175: - Ask whether to overwrite or create a versioned copy (`review_{tag}_v2.md`)
176: - If overwriting, preserve the previous version's content in a collapsible section at the bottom
177: 
178: ### Step 9: Report
179: 
180: Present a summary to the user:
181: 
182: ```text
183: Review: {Title}
184:   Conference: {Conference}
185:   Notes:    knowledge/inbox/summary_{tag}.md
186:   Review:   knowledge/reviews/review_{tag}.md
187: 
188:   Rating: {score}/{max} — {score meaning from rubric}
189:   Confidence: {score}/5
190: 
191:   Top strength:  {best strength, one sentence}
192:   Top weakness:  {most critical weakness, one sentence}
193: 
194:   Follow up:
195:   /rebuttal {tag} "rebuttal text"  — submit a rebuttal
196:   /rebuttal {tag} "re-read Section 4"  — request deeper analysis
197: ```
198: 
199: ## Key Rules
200: 
201: - Reviews must demonstrate deep understanding, not surface-level impressions
202: - Every strength and weakness must cite specific equations, theorems, tables, or figures
203: - Do not give generic feedback ("the writing could be improved") — be specific about what and where
204: - The rating must be justified by the evidence in the review, not arbitrary
205: - If you are unsure about a technical claim, state it as a question rather than a weakness
206: - Re-read the original source for depth — do not rely solely on the reading notes
207: - Use Gemini for figure analysis — figures contain critical information for review
208: - Follow the conference template exactly — each conference has different required sections and scoring
209: - Be constructive: every weakness should have a corresponding suggestion for improvement
210: - If the paper has an arXiv ID and was read from PDF, suggest re-reading from LaTeX source for higher quality

(End of file - total 210 lines)
</content>

1: ---
2: name: rebuttal
3: description: Handle author rebuttals for a previously reviewed paper. Re-reads relevant sections, evaluates rebuttal validity against paper content, and updates the review with [Updated after rebuttal] markers. Also handles re-read requests and clarifying questions.
4: argument-hint: [tag] [rebuttal-text-or-instruction]
5: allowed-tools: Bash(*), Read, Grep, Glob, Write
6: ---
7: 
8: # Rebuttal — Iterative Review Update
9: 
10: Rebuttal: $ARGUMENTS
11: 
12: > The review is a living document. Updates preserve original text and add `[Updated after rebuttal]` markers.
13: 
14: ## Constants
15: 
16: - **KNOWLEDGE_REVIEWS** — `knowledge/reviews/` — review outputs
17: - **PAPERS_INBOX** — `papers/inbox/` — original paper source files
18: - **GEMINI_SCRIPT** — `tools/gemini_vision.py` relative to project root
19: 
20: ## Workflow
21: 
22: ### Step 1: Parse Arguments
23: 
24: Parse `$ARGUMENTS` for:
25: 
26: - **Tag**: the paper's tag (used to find the review)
27: - **Instruction**: one of the following types:
28:   - **Rebuttal text**: "Our method handles non-stationary budgets through adaptive thresholds (Section 4.2), which the reviewer may have overlooked."
29:   - **Re-read request**: "re-read Section 4.2" or "re-read the appendix proofs"
30:   - **Question**: "What exactly does Equation 3 imply for the loss landscape?"
31:   - **Score adjustment**: "Adjust rating to 7 given the rebuttal"
32: 
33: Detect the instruction type by analyzing the content:
34: - If it mentions "re-read" or specifies a section → re-read request
35: - If it starts with a question word or "?" → question
36: - If it argues against a specific weakness → rebuttal
37: - If it mentions "adjust", "change", "update rating" → score adjustment
38: 
39: ### Step 2: Locate the Review
40: 
41: Find the existing review:
42: 
43: 1. Check `knowledge/reviews/review_{tag}.md`
44: 2. If not found, check for versioned copies: `review_{tag}_v2.md`, `review_{tag}_v3.md` (use the latest)
45: 3. If no review exists, error out:
46: 
47: ```text
48: No review found for tag "{tag}". Please create a review first:
49:   /review {tag} CONFERENCE
50: ```
51: 
52: Read the entire review file.
53: 
54: ### Step 3: Locate Paper Source
55: 
56: From the review, determine the paper source:
57: 
58: - The review header should contain the paper title and source information
59: - Locate the original notes in `knowledge/inbox/summary_{tag}.md` or `knowledge/archive/summary_{tag}.md`
60: - Locate the source files in `papers/inbox/` (LaTeX or PDF-derived Markdown)
61: 
62: ### Step 4: Re-read Relevant Sections
63: 
64: Based on the instruction type:
65: 
66: **For rebuttals**:
67: - Identify which weaknesses/questions the rebuttal addresses
68: - Re-read the paper sections those weaknesses reference (e.g., if the weakness cites "Equation 5 in Section 3.2", re-read Section 3.2)
69: - If the rebuttal references a specific section, equation, or experiment, re-read that part of the original source
70: 
71: **For re-read requests**:
72: - Parse the section reference from the instruction
73: - Re-read that section from the original source (LaTeX .tex files or PDF Markdown)
74: - If no specific section is given, re-read the Method and Experiments sections
75: 
76: **For questions**:
77: - Identify which part of the paper the question relates to
78: - Re-read that section to answer accurately
79: - Do not guess — verify against the actual paper content
80: 
81: **For Gemini re-analysis** (if the rebuttal discusses figures):
82: - Re-analyze the relevant figures with Gemini, possibly with a more targeted prompt
83: - Example: "The authors claim Figure 3 shows monotonic improvement. Verify this claim."
84: 
85: ### Step 5: Evaluate Rebuttal
86: 
87: For each point in the rebuttal:
88: 
89: 1. **Check against paper content**: Does the rebuttal accurately represent what the paper says?
90: 2. **Check against the original weakness**: Does the rebuttal actually address the weakness raised?
91: 3. **Classify each point**:
92:    - **VALID**: Rebuttal correctly addresses the weakness with evidence from the paper. The weakness may be partially or fully resolved.
93:    - **PARTIALLY VALID**: Rebuttal addresses some aspect but doesn't fully resolve the concern. The weakness is mitigated but not eliminated.
94:    - **INVALID**: Rebuttal misrepresents paper content, doesn't address the actual weakness, or introduces new issues.
95: 
96: 4. **Assess score impact**:
97:    - If one or more VALID points resolve key weaknesses → consider raising the rating
98:    - If rebuttal reveals new issues not caught in initial review → consider lowering the rating
99:    - If rebuttal is only PARTIALLY VALID → rating may stay the same with updated justification
100: 
101: ### Step 6: Update the Review
102: 
103: For each rebuttal point, update the corresponding section in the review:
104: 
105: **Weakness update format**:
106: 
107: ```markdown
108: 1. **Original weakness**: [original text]
109: 
110:    [Updated after rebuttal]
111:    - **Rebuttal claim**: [what the authors argue]
112:    - **Assessment**: VALID / PARTIALLY VALID / INVALID
113:    - **Reasoning**: [evaluation against paper content, citing specific evidence]
114:    - **Score impact**: [whether this changes the rating, and why]
115: ```
116: 
117: **Question update format** (if the rebuttal answers a question):
118: 
119: ```markdown
120: 3. **Original question**: [original text]
121: 
122:    [Updated after rebuttal]
123:    - **Author response**: [summary of their answer]
124:    - **Assessment**: [whether the answer is satisfactory]
125: ```
126: 
127: **Rating update** (if warranted):
128: 
129: ```markdown
130: ## Rating
131: 
132: **Score**: [new score]
133: 
134: [Rating updated: {old_score} → {new_score} after rebuttal]
135: 
136: [Updated justification paragraph incorporating rebuttal findings]
137: ```
138: 
139: **Add a Rebuttal Summary section** at the end of the review:
140: 
141: ```markdown
142: ## Rebuttal Summary
143: 
144: **Rebuttal received**: [date or "session-based"]
145: 
146: | Point | Assessment | Score Impact |
147: |-------|-----------|--------------|
148: | [Rebuttal point 1] | VALID | +0.5 |
149: | [Rebuttal point 2] | PARTIALLY VALID | 0 |
150: | [Rebuttal point 3] | INVALID | 0 |
151: 
152: **Net rating change**: {old_score} → {new_score} ({delta})
153: ```
154: 
155: ### Step 7: Save Updated Review
156: 
157: Overwrite `knowledge/reviews/review_{tag}.md` with the updated version.
158: 
159: Preserve the complete original review text within `[Updated after rebuttal]` blocks — never delete the original critique.
160: 
161: ### Step 8: Report
162: 
163: Present the update summary to the user:
164: 
165: ```text
166: Rebuttal processed for: {Title}
167:   Review:  knowledge/reviews/review_{tag}.md
168: 
169:   Points evaluated: {N}
170:   VALID:            {n_valid}
171:   PARTIALLY VALID:  {n_partial}
172:   INVALID:          {n_invalid}
173: 
174:   Rating: {old_score} → {new_score} ({delta})
175: 
176:   Key changes:
177:   - [Summary of most significant update]
178:   - [Summary of second most significant update]
179: 
180:   Follow up:
181:   /rebuttal {tag} "another point"  — submit another rebuttal point
182:   /rebuttal {tag} "re-read Section X"  — request deeper analysis
183: ```
184: 
185: ## Key Rules
186: 
187: - Always re-read the relevant paper sections before evaluating a rebuttal — do not rely on memory or notes alone
188: - Original review text must be preserved — updates are additive, not destructive
189: - Use `[Updated after rebuttal]` markers clearly for every change
190: - Be fair in evaluation: a valid rebuttal should lead to an updated weakness assessment
191: - Do not automatically raise ratings — only adjust if the rebuttal genuinely resolves a concern
192: - A rebuttal that introduces new issues can lower the rating
193: - Multiple rebuttal rounds are supported — each round adds new `[Updated after rebuttal]` sections
194: - If the user asks a question about the paper, answer it by re-reading the source — do not guess
195: - If Gemini figure re-analysis is needed, use it — but only for visual content, not text understanding

1: ---
2: name: read-paper
3: description: Deep-read an arXiv paper from its LaTeX source. Downloads .tex source, locates the entrypoint, recursively reads all section files, and produces structured notes saved to knowledge/. Use when user says "read this paper", "read paper", gives an arXiv URL/ID, or wants detailed notes on a specific paper. PDF IS BANNED — only LaTeX source.
4: argument-hint: [arxiv-url-or-id]
5: allowed-tools: Bash(*), Read, Grep, Glob, Write
6: ---
7: 
8: # Read Paper — Deep LaTeX Source Reading
9: 
10: Read paper: $ARGUMENTS
11: 
12: > **CRITICAL RULE: PDF IS BANNED.** Never download, link to, or suggest PDF files.
13: > All reading is done from local .tex source files only.
14: 
15: ## Constants
16: 
17: - **PAPER_DIR** — `papers/inbox/` relative to project root. Newly downloaded LaTeX source goes here.
18: - **KNOWLEDGE_DIR** — `knowledge/inbox/` relative to project root. Structured notes for new papers go here.
19: - **FETCH_SCRIPT** — `tools/arxiv_fetch.py` relative to project root.
20: 
21: > Overrides (append to arguments):
22: > - `/read-paper 2301.07041 - focus: method` — emphasize methodology in notes
23: > - `/read-paper 2301.07041 - focus: experiments` — emphasize experimental results
24: > - `/read-paper 2301.07041 - focus: insights` — emphasize key insights and connections
25: > - `/read-paper 2301.07041 - tag: custom_tag` — override the auto-generated note tag
26: > - `/read-paper 2301.07041 - no-download` — skip download, assume source already exists locally
27: 
28: ## Workflow
29: 
30: ### Step 1: Parse Arguments
31: 
32: Parse `$ARGUMENTS` for:
33: 
34: - **arXiv ID or URL**: e.g. `2601.07372` or `https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.07372`
35: - **`- focus: AREA`**: reading emphasis — `method` / `experiments` / `insights` (default: balanced)
36: - **`- tag: NAME`**: override the auto-generated tag for the note file
37: - **`- no-download`**: skip download step, assume .tex source is already in `papers/inbox/`
38: 
39: Normalize the ID:
40: - Strip URL prefix: `https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.07041` → `2301.07041`
41: - Strip version suffix: `2301.07041v2` → `2301.07041`
42: - Validate format: should match `YYMM.NNNNN` or `category/NNNNNNN`
43: 
44: ### Step 2: Download LaTeX Source
45: 
46: Unless `- no-download` is specified:
47: 
48: ```bash
49: python3 tools/arxiv_fetch.py download ARXIV_ID
50: ```
51: 
52: This downloads from `https://arxiv.org/e-print/ARXIV_ID`, extracts to `papers/inbox/ARXIV_ID/`, and lists all .tex files.
53: 
54: If the download script places files in `papers/ARXIV_ID/` (old behavior), move them to `papers/inbox/ARXIV_ID/` after download.
55: 
56: If the directory already exists with .tex files, the script will skip the download.
57: 
58: If download fails (no LaTeX source available), report it clearly and stop — this paper cannot be read with this skill.
59: 
60: ### Step 3: Locate the Entrypoint
61: 
62: Find the main .tex file that contains `\documentclass`:
63: 
64: ```bash
65: grep -rl '\\documentclass' papers/inbox/ARXIV_ID/
66: ```
67: 
68: Common entrypoint names: `main.tex`, `paper.tex`, `ms.tex`, `acl_latex.tex`, `colm2026_conference.tex`, etc.
69: 
70: The entrypoint is the root of the document — it contains `\begin{document}` and pulls in all other files via `\input{}` or `\include{}`.
71: 
72: ### Step 4: Map the Document Structure
73: 
74: From the entrypoint, extract the `\input{}` and `\include{}` directives to build the reading order:
75: 
76: ```bash
77: grep -n '\\input\|\\include' papers/inbox/ARXIV_ID/ENTRYPOINT.tex
78: ```
79: 
80: This gives the logical order of sections. Typical structure:
81: 
82: ```text
83: \input{sections/introduction}
84: \input{sections/preliminary}
85: \input{sections/method}
86: \input{sections/experiment}
87: \input{sections/related}
88: \input{sections/conclusion}
89: \input{sections/appendix}
90: ```
91: 
92: Resolve each input path to an actual file:
93: - `\input{sections/method}` → `sections/method.tex`
94: - `\input{Sections/1-Introduction}` → `Sections/1-Introduction.tex`
95: - Some papers include the `.tex` extension explicitly
96: 
97: Build a reading list in document order.
98: 
99: ### Step 5: Read the Paper
100: 
101: Read each file in the reading list using the Read tool. For each file:
102: 
103: 1. **Metadata (from entrypoint)**: Extract title, authors, abstract, date
104: 2. **Introduction**: Problem statement, motivation, contributions
105: 3. **Background/Preliminary**: Key definitions, notation, prior work needed
106: 4. **Method**: Core approach, architecture, algorithms, key equations
107: 5. **Experiments**: Setup, datasets, baselines, main results
108: 6. **Related Work**: Positioning against existing work
109: 7. **Conclusion**: Summary, limitations, future work
110: 8. **Appendix** (if relevant): Additional details, proofs, extra experiments
111: 
112: **Reading strategy**:
113: 
114: - Read the entrypoint first for metadata and overall structure
115: - Then read each section file in order
116: - For equations: search for `\begin{equation}`, `\begin{align}`, `\begin{algorithm}` — note key formulas
117: - For tables: search for `\begin{table}` — note main results
118: - For figures: note captions from `\caption{}` — they often contain key findings
119: - Skip pure formatting/boilerplate content (style definitions, bibliography)
120: 
121: **If the paper is very long** (many sections, appendix hundreds of lines):
122: - Read the core sections fully (intro, method, experiments, conclusion)
123: - For appendix: scan headings and read only sections directly relevant to the focus area
124: - Note which appendix sections were skipped for transparency
125: 
126: ### Step 5.5: Gemini Figure Analysis
127: 
128: Text understanding stays with Claude Code, but figures/diagrams need visual analysis. Use Gemini for this.
129: 
130: 1. **Find all figure references** in the .tex source:
131: 
132: ```bash
133: grep -rn '\\includegraphics' papers/inbox/ARXIV_ID/
134: ```
135: 
136: 2. **Collect image paths**: For each `\includegraphics{path}`, resolve the actual file path relative to the paper directory. Handle missing extensions (try `.pdf`, `.png`, `.jpg`, `.eps`).
137: 
138: 3. **Analyze each figure** with Gemini:
139: 
140: ```bash
141: uv run python tools/gemini_vision.py analyze "papers/inbox/ARXIV_ID/figures/fig1.pdf" --prompt "Describe this figure from an academic paper. What does it show? What are the axes, data series, and key takeaway?"
142: ```
143: 
144: For efficiency, batch related figures:
145: 
146: ```bash
147: uv run python tools/gemini_vision.py analyze fig1.pdf fig2.pdf --prompt "Describe each figure separately."
148: ```
149: 
150: 4. **Incorporate results**: Add a `### Figures` subsection under Method and Experiments sections in the notes, with Gemini's descriptions of each figure.
151: 
152: 5. **If no images found** (some papers have no figures, or figures are TikZ-only), skip this step and note "No raster/vector images found — figures may be TikZ inline drawings."
153: 
154: > **Rate limit**: Add a 1-second pause between consecutive Gemini API calls to respect quotas.
155: 
156: ### Step 6: Generate Structured Notes
157: 
158: Produce a structured markdown note and save to `knowledge/inbox/summary_{tag}.md`.
159: 
160: **Tag generation**: Use a short descriptive tag derived from the paper's key concept:
161: - "Attention Is All You Need" → `self_attention`
162: - "ClawGuard: Runtime Security..." → `agent_security`
163: - "AggAgent: Agentic Aggregation..." → `agentic_aggregation`
164: - Keep tags lowercase, underscore-separated, 2-4 words max
165: - If `- tag: NAME` override was provided, use that
166: - Check that the file doesn't already exist — never overwrite
167: 
168: **Note template**:
169: 
170: ```markdown
171: # {Title}
172: 
173: - **arXiv**: [{ID}](https://arxiv.org/abs/{ID})
174: - **Authors**: {first_author} et al. ({total_authors} authors)
175: - **Date**: {published_date}
176: - **Tag**: {tag}
177: 
178: ## Problem
179: 
180: {What problem does this paper address? What gap in existing work? 2-4 sentences.}
181: 
182: ## Method
183: 
184: {Core approach in 4-8 sentences. Include:
185: - Key idea / insight
186: - Architecture or algorithm overview
187: - Notable design decisions}
188: 
189: ### Key Equations
190: 
191: {List 1-3 most important equations or algorithms with brief explanation of what each represents. Use LaTeX notation.}
192: 
193: ### Figures
194: 
195: {If Gemini analysis was performed, list each figure with:
196: - **Figure N**: {caption} — {Gemini description of visual content and key takeaway}
197: If no figures or TikZ-only, write: "No raster images found — figures may be inline TikZ drawings."}
198: 
199: ## Experiments
200: 
201: ### Setup
202: 
203: - **Benchmarks/Datasets**: {list}
204: - **Baselines**: {list}
205: - **Metrics**: {list}
206: - **Models tested**: {list}
207: 
208: ### Main Results
209: 
210: {Key findings in 2-4 sentences. Reference specific numbers from tables.}
211: 
212: ## Key Insights
213: 
214: {2-5 bullet points capturing the most important takeaways:
215: - What did we learn that wasn't obvious before?
216: - What design choices matter and why?
217: - What are the limitations or caveats?}
218: 
219: ## Connections
220: 
221: {How does this relate to other work? Identify:
222: - Builds on: {prior work this extends}
223: - Related to: {contemporary work on similar problems}
224: - Enables: {what future work this could support}
225: - Contrasts with: {work that takes a different approach}}
226: 
227: ## Questions for Further Exploration
228: 
229: {2-3 open questions this paper raises or doesn't fully answer}
230: 
231: ---
232: *Generated by read-paper skill from LaTeX source*
233: *Source: papers/{ID}/*
234: ```
235: 
236: **Focus adjustments**:
237: 
238: When `- focus: AREA` is specified:
239: 
240: - **`method`**: Expand Method section significantly, add more equations and algorithmic details, trim Experiments
241: - **`experiments`**: Expand Experiments with full result tables, ablation studies, analysis; trim Method
242: - **`insights`**: Expand Key Insights and Connections, add a "Broader Impact" subsection; trim technical details
243: 
244: ### Step 7: Report
245: 
246: Present a brief summary to the user:
247: 
248: ```text
249: Read {Title} ({ID})
250:   Source: papers/inbox/{ID}/ ({N} .tex files)
251:   Notes:  knowledge/inbox/summary_{tag}.md
252: 
253:   Problem: {one sentence}
254:   Method:  {one sentence}
255:   Result:  {one sentence with key number}
256: 
257:   Key insight: {most important takeaway}
258: ```
259: 
260: Suggest follow-up:
261: 
262: ```text
263: /daily-papers              — discover more papers
264: /read-paper ANOTHER_ID     — read another paper
265: (Coming: /synthesize)       — cross-paper analysis and insight formation
266: ```
267: 
268: ## Key Rules
269: 
270: - **PDF IS BANNED.** Never download, link to, or suggest PDF files.
271: - Always download LaTeX source first (unless `- no-download`). Reading abstract-only is not acceptable for this skill.
272: - The note file must be saved to `knowledge/inbox/summary_{tag}.md`, NOT inside `papers/` or `~/.cache/`.
273: - Never overwrite an existing note file — pick a different tag if needed.
274: - Read ALL core sections (intro through conclusion). Skipping sections is only allowed for appendix content.
275: - Extract actual content from .tex — don't just summarize the abstract. The whole point is deep reading.
276: - For equations: preserve LaTeX notation in notes so they remain readable.
277: - For tables: extract key numbers, don't just say "see Table 1".
278: - The "Connections" section is critical for future synthesize skill — always fill it thoughtfully.
279: - If a .tex file is empty or contains only boilerplate, skip it and note that.
280: - Handle papers where `\input` paths don't match actual files gracefully — glob for the closest match.
281: - Chinese comments in .tex source should be noted (some authors leave implementation notes in comments).

7: 
8: # Read PDF — Deep PDF Reading via docling
9: 
10: Read PDF: $ARGUMENTS
11: 
12: > **Local PDFs are allowed.** This skill reads local PDF files via docling extraction.
13: > Remote PDF download is still banned — only local file paths accepted.
14: > If the paper has an arXiv ID, LaTeX source via `/read-paper` provides higher quality.
15: 
16: ## Constants
17: 
18: - **PAPER_DIR** — `papers/inbox/` relative to project root. Parsed Markdown goes here.
19: - **KNOWLEDGE_DIR** — `knowledge/inbox/` relative to project root. Structured notes go here.
20: - **PDF_SCRIPT** — `tools/pdf_parse.py` relative to project root.
21: 
22: > Overrides (append to arguments):
23: > - `/read-pdf /path/to/paper.pdf - focus: method` — emphasize methodology in notes
24: > - `/read-pdf /path/to/paper.pdf - focus: experiments` — emphasize experimental results
25: > - `/read-pdf /path/to/paper.pdf - focus: insights` — emphasize key insights and connections
26: > - `/read-pdf /path/to/paper.pdf - tag: custom_tag` — override the auto-generated note tag
27: 
28: ## Workflow
29: 
30: ### Step 1: Parse Arguments
31: 
32: Parse `$ARGUMENTS` for:
33: 
34: - **PDF path**: local file path ending in `.pdf` (must exist on disk)
35: - **`- focus: AREA`**: reading emphasis — `method` / `experiments` / `insights` (default: balanced)
36: - **`- tag: NAME`**: override the auto-generated tag for the note file
37: 
38: Validate: path exists, file has `.pdf` extension.
39: 
40: ### Step 2: Detect arXiv ID
41: 
42: Before parsing, check the PDF filename for arXiv ID patterns (`YYMM.NNNNN` or `category/NNNNNNN`).
43: 
44: If an arXiv ID is found, inform the user:
45: 
46: > "This paper appears to be from arXiv (ID: {id}). LaTeX source provides higher quality reading. Use `/read-paper {id}` instead, or confirm to proceed with PDF."
47: 
48: Wait for user confirmation before proceeding. If no arXiv ID is detected in the filename, proceed directly.
49: 
50: After metadata extraction (Step 3), also check the extracted metadata for an `arxiv_id` field. If found and not previously detected, note it in the report but do not interrupt.
51: 
52: ### Step 3: Parse the PDF
53: 
54: Parse the PDF using docling:
55: 
56: ```bash
57: uv run python tools/pdf_parse.py parse "PATH/TO/paper.pdf" --dir papers/inbox
58: ```
59: 
60: This outputs JSON to stdout with:
61: 
62: ```json
63: {
64:   "slug": "attention-is-all-you-need",
65:   "output_dir": "papers/inbox/attention-is-all-you-need",
66:   "markdown_file": "papers/inbox/attention-is-all-you-need/paper.md",
67:   "metadata_file": "papers/inbox/attention-is-all-you-need/metadata.json",
68:   "metadata": {
69:     "title": "...",
70:     "authors": [{"full": "..."}],
71:     "date": "...",
72:     "arxiv_id": "...",
73:     "source_type": "pdf"
74:   }
75: }
76: ```
77: 
78: Parse the JSON to get the Markdown file path and metadata.
79: 
80: If parsing fails (docling error, malformed PDF), report the error clearly and suggest:
81: - Trying a different PDF version
82: - Using the paper's arXiv ID with `/read-paper` if available
83: 
84: ### Step 3.5: Gemini Figure Analysis
85: 
86: docling extraction preserves captions but loses figure content. Use Gemini to analyze figures visually.
87: 
88: 1. **Upload the PDF to Gemini** and ask about all figures:
89: 
90: ```bash
91: uv run python tools/gemini_vision.py pdf-figures "PATH/TO/paper.pdf"
92: ```
93: 
94: This uploads the PDF via Gemini's File API and returns a JSON with figure descriptions (figure ID, caption, description, key takeaway).
95: 
96: 2. **Parse the JSON output** and extract figure descriptions.
97: 
98: 3. **If Gemini fails** (API error, timeout), note "Figure analysis unavailable — Gemini API error" and continue without it. The review can still reference captions.
99: 
100: > **Note**: The original PDF path must be accessible for this step. If the PDF was moved or deleted after parsing, skip this step.
101: 
102: ### Step 4: Read the Paper
103: 
104: Read the generated Markdown file using the Read tool. The Markdown from docling includes:
105: 
106: - Section headings (`## Section Name`) preserving the paper's structure
107: - Paragraph text with reasonable formatting
108: - Tables converted to Markdown where possible
109: - Figure/image placeholders with captions
110: 
111: Reading strategy:
112: 
113: 1. Read `metadata.json` first for structured author/date/arXiv ID info
114: 2. Read `paper.md` — for longer papers, read in sections if the file is large
115: 3. For figures: note any image/figure placeholders and their captions
116: 4. For tables: extract key numbers from the Markdown tables
117: 
118: **Quality caveat**: docling's extraction is good but not perfect compared to LaTeX source. Some equations may not render correctly, and figure content is lost (only captions preserved). Note any sections where extraction quality seems poor.
119: 
120: ### Step 5: Generate Structured Notes
121: 
122: Produce a structured markdown note and save to `knowledge/inbox/summary_{tag}.md`.
123: 
124: **Tag generation**: Use a short descriptive tag derived from the paper's key concept:
125: - "Attention Is All You Need" → `self_attention`
126: - Keep tags lowercase, underscore-separated, 2-4 words max
127: - If the metadata has an `arxiv_id`, use `summary_{arxiv_id_with_underscores}` for consistency with `/read-paper` (e.g., `summary_2604_12782`)
128: - If `- tag: NAME` override was provided, use that
129: - Check that the file doesn't already exist — never overwrite
130: 
131: **Note template**:
132: 
133: ```markdown
134: # {Title}
135: 
136: - **Source**: PDF (via docling)
137: - **PDF path**: {original_pdf_path}
138: - **Authors**: {first_author} et al. ({total_authors} authors)
139: - **Date**: {date}
140: - **Tag**: {tag}
141: {arXiv_id_line}
142: 
143: ## Problem
144: 
145: {What problem does this paper address? What gap in existing work? 2-4 sentences.}
146: 
147: ## Method
148: 
149: {Core approach in 4-8 sentences. Include:
150: - Key idea / insight
151: - Architecture or algorithm overview
152: - Notable design decisions}
153: 
154: ### Key Equations
155: 
156: {List 1-3 most important equations or algorithms with brief explanation of what each represents. Use LaTeX notation. If equations were not fully extractable from PDF, note: "Equations not fully extractable from PDF — see original paper."}
157: 
158: ### Figures
159: 
160: {If Gemini analysis was performed, list each figure with:
161: - **Figure N**: {caption} — {Gemini description of visual content and key takeaway}
162: If Gemini analysis was skipped: "Figure visual analysis not available — captions only."}
163: 
164: ## Experiments
165: 
166: ### Setup
167:

1: ---
2: name: arxiv
3: description: Search arXiv and download LaTeX source for academic papers. Use when user says "search arxiv", "download paper", "fetch arxiv", "arxiv search", "get paper source", or wants to find and read papers from arXiv. PDF download is BANNED — only LaTeX source (.tar.gz) is allowed.
4: argument-hint: [query-or-arxiv-id]
5: allowed-tools: Bash(*), Read, Grep, Glob, Write
6: ---
7: 
8: # arXiv Paper Search & LaTeX Source Download
9: 
10: Search topic or arXiv paper ID: $ARGUMENTS
11: 
12: > **CRITICAL RULE: PDF is BANNED.** Never download, fetch, or link to PDF files.
13: > Only download LaTeX source archives from `https://arxiv.org/e-print/ID`.
14: > LaTeX source can be searched with Grep, read section-by-section with Read,
15: > and analyzed far more effectively than PDF.
16: 
17: ## Constants
18: 
19: - **PAPER_DIR** - Local directory to save downloaded LaTeX source. Default: `papers/inbox/` in the current project directory (dual-zone: inbox for new papers, archive for synthesized papers).
20: - **MAX_RESULTS = 10** - Default number of search results.
21: - **FETCH_SCRIPT** - `tools/arxiv_fetch.py` relative to the project root. Fall back to inline Python if not found.
22: 
23: > Overrides (append to arguments):
24: > - `/arxiv "attention mechanism" - max: 20` - return up to 20 results
25: > - `/arxiv "2301.07041" - download` - download LaTeX source for a specific paper by ID
26: > - `/arxiv "query" - dir: literature/` - save source to a custom directory
27: > - `/arxiv "query" - download: all` - download source for all results
28: 
29: ## Workflow
30: 
31: ### Step 1: Parse Arguments
32: 
33: Parse `$ARGUMENTS` for directives:
34: 
35: - **Query or ID**: main search term or a bare arXiv ID such as `2301.07041` or `cs/0601001`
36: - **`- max: N`**: override MAX_RESULTS (e.g., `- max: 20`)
37: - **`- dir: PATH`**: override PAPER_DIR (e.g., `- dir: literature/`)
38: - **`- download`**: download the first result's LaTeX source after listing
39: - **`- download: all`**: download LaTeX source for all results
40: 
41: If the argument matches an arXiv ID pattern (`YYMM.NNNNN` or `category/NNNNNNN`), skip the search and go directly to Step 3.
42: 
43: ### Step 2: Search arXiv
44: 
45: Locate the fetch script:
46: 
47: ```bash
48: SCRIPT=$(find tools/ -name "arxiv_fetch.py" 2>/dev/null | head -1)
49: ```
50: 
51: **If SCRIPT is found**, run:
52: 
53: ```bash
54: python3 "$SCRIPT" search "QUERY" --max MAX_RESULTS
55: ```
56: 
57: **If SCRIPT is not found**, fall back to inline Python:
58: 
59: ```bash
60: python3 - <<'PYEOF'
61: import json
62: import urllib.parse
63: import urllib.request
64: import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
65: 
66: NS = "http://www.w3.org/2005/Atom"
67: query = urllib.parse.quote("QUERY")
68: url = (f"http://export.arxiv.org/api/query"
69:        f"?search_query={query}&start=0&max_results=MAX_RESULTS"
70:        f"&sortBy=relevance&sortOrder=descending")
71: with urllib.request.urlopen(url, timeout=30) as r:
72:     root = ET.fromstring(r.read())
73: papers = []
74: for entry in root.findall(f"{{{NS}}}entry"):
75:     aid = entry.findtext(f"{{{NS}}}id", "").split("/abs/")[-1].split("v")[0]
76:     title = (entry.findtext(f"{{{NS}}}title", "") or "").strip().replace("\n", " ")
77:     abstract = (entry.findtext(f"{{{NS}}}summary", "") or "").strip().replace("\n", " ")
78:     authors = [a.findtext(f"{{{NS}}}name", "") for a in entry.findall(f"{{{NS}}}author")]
79:     published = entry.findtext(f"{{{NS}}}published", "")[:10]
80:     cats = [c.get("term", "") for c in entry.findall(f"{{{NS}}}category")]
81:     papers.append({
82:         "id": aid,
83:         "title": title,
84:         "authors": authors,
85:         "abstract": abstract,
86:         "published": published,
87:         "categories": cats,
88:         "abs_url": f"https://arxiv.org/abs/{aid}",
89:         "source_url": f"https://arxiv.org/e-print/{aid}",
90:     })
91: print(json.dumps(papers, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2))
92: PYEOF
93: ```
94: 
95: Present results as a table:
96: 
97: ```text
98: | # | arXiv ID   | Title               | Authors        | Date       | Category |
99: |---|------------|---------------------|----------------|------------|----------|
100: | 1 | 2301.07041 | Attention Is All... | Vaswani et al. | 2017-06-12 | cs.LG    |
101: ```
102: 
103: ### Step 3: Fetch Details for a Specific ID
104: 
105: When a single paper ID is requested (either directly or from Step 2):
106: 
107: ```bash
108: python3 "$SCRIPT" paper "ARXIV_ID"
109: ```
110: 
111: Display: title, all authors, categories, full abstract, published date, abstract URL, source URL.
112: 
113: ### Step 4: Download LaTeX Source
114: 
115: When download is requested, for each paper ID to download:
116: 
117: ```bash
118: # Using fetch script:
119: python3 "$SCRIPT" download ARXIV_ID --dir PAPER_DIR
120: ```
121: 
122: The script will:
123: 1. Download `https://arxiv.org/e-print/ARXIV_ID` (LaTeX source archive)
124: 2. Extract to `PAPER_DIR/ARXIV_ID/`
125: 3. List all `.tex` files found with line counts
126: 
127: After each download:
128: 
129: - Confirm the extracted directory contains `.tex` files
130: - If no LaTeX source is available (404 — scanned/older paper), report it clearly
131: - Add a 1-second delay between consecutive downloads to avoid rate limiting
132: - Report: `Downloaded: papers/inbox/2301.07041/ (N .tex files)`
133: 
134: **Once downloaded, read the paper using Read and Grep on the .tex files:**
135: 
136: ```bash
137: # Find the main .tex file (usually main.tex, ms.tex, or paper.tex)
138: # Read the full paper
139: Read: papers/inbox/2301.07041/main.tex
140: 
141: # Search for specific content
142: Grep: "attention" in papers/inbox/2301.07041/
143: 
144: # Read a specific section
145: Read: papers/inbox/2301.07041/experiments.tex
146: ```
147: 
148: ### Step 5: Summarize
149: 
150: For each paper (downloaded or fetched by API):
151: 
152: ```markdown
153: ## [Title]
154: 
155: - **arXiv**: [ID] - [abs_url]
156: - **Authors**: [full author list]
157: - **Date**: [published]
158: - **Categories**: [cs.LG, cs.AI, ...]
159: - **Abstract**: [full abstract]
160: - **Key contributions** (extracted from abstract):
161:   - [contribution 1]
162:   - [contribution 2]
163:   - [contribution 3]
164: - **LaTeX source**: papers/inbox/[ID]/ (if downloaded — read .tex files for full content)
165: ```
166: 
167: If LaTeX source was downloaded, additionally extract from the .tex files:
168: - Methodology details (from method/approach sections)
169: - Key equations (search for `\begin{equation}`, `\begin{align}`)
170: - Experimental setup (from experiments section)
171: - Results tables (search for `\begin{table}`)
172: 
173: ### Step 6: Final Output
174: 
175: Summarize what was done:
176: 
177: - `Found N papers for "query"`
178: - `Downloaded source: papers/inbox/2301.07041/ (N .tex files)` (for each download)
179: - Any warnings (rate limit hit, no LaTeX source available, already exists)
180: 
181: Suggest follow-up skills:
182: 
183: ```text
184: /read-paper ARXIV_ID      - deep-read this paper from LaTeX source
185: /search-knowledge "topic"  - search across all read paper notes
186: ```
187: 
188: ## Key Rules
189: 
190: - **PDF IS BANNED.** Never download, link to, or suggest PDF files. Only use LaTeX source from `https://arxiv.org/e-print/ID`.
191: - Always show the arXiv ID prominently - users need it for citations and reproducibility
192: - Verify downloaded source: directory must contain at least one `.tex` file
193: - Rate limit: wait 1 second between consecutive source downloads; retry once after 5 seconds on HTTP 429
194: - Never overwrite an existing source directory - skip it and report "already exists"
195: - Handle both arXiv ID formats: new (`2301.07041`) and old (`cs/0601001`)
196: - PAPER_DIR is created automatically if it does not exist
197: - If a paper has no LaTeX source (scanned/older papers), report this clearly — this paper cannot be read with `/read-paper`
198: - If the arXiv API is unreachable, report the error clearly and suggest trying again later
199: - When LaTeX source is available, always prefer reading .tex files over abstract-only summaries

(End of file - total 199 lines)

1: # NeurIPS Conference Review Format
2: 
3: ## Conference
4: NeurIPS (Neural Information Processing Systems)
5: 
6: ## Review Criteria
7: Papers are evaluated on quality, clarity, originality, and significance.
8: 
9: ## Scoring Rubric
10: 
11: ### Rating (1-10)
12: | Score | Meaning |
13: |-------|---------|
14: | 1 | Trivial or wrong — no significant contribution |
15: | 2 | Very weak rejection — major flaws, minimal contribution |
16: | 3 | Clear rejection — significant issues outweigh merits |
17: | 4 | Weak rejection — issues present but some merit |
18: | 5 | Marginally below acceptance threshold |
19: | 6 | Marginally above acceptance threshold |
20: | 7 | Weak accept — good paper with minor issues |
21: | 8 | Clear accept — strong contribution |
22: | 9 | Strong accept — excellent paper |
23: | 10 | Top 5% paper, standout contribution |
24: 
25: ### Confidence (1-5)
26: | Score | Meaning |
27: |-------|---------|
28: | 1 | Low — not my area, hard to judge |
29: | 2 | Marginal — read abstract and skimmed |
30: | 3 | Medium — familiar with the area |
31: | 4 | High — expert in the area |
32: | 5 | Very high — I wrote papers on this exact topic |
33: 
34: ## Required Sections
35: 
36: 1. **Summary** — Brief summary of the paper (3-5 sentences), demonstrating deep understanding of the core contribution
37: 2. **Strengths** — Specific strengths with evidence from the paper (cite equations, theorems, experimental results)
38: 3. **Weaknesses** — Specific weaknesses with evidence (cite specific equations, theorems, tables, figures)
39: 4. **Questions for Authors** — Clarifying questions that show deep engagement with the paper
40: 5. **Limitations** — Identified limitations and assumptions
41: 6. **Ethics Concerns** — Potential ethical issues (write "N/A" if none)
42: 7. **Reproducibility** — Whether the paper provides sufficient details for reproduction
43: 8. **Rating** — Numerical score (1-10) with a justification paragraph
44: 9. **Confidence** — Confidence score (1-5) with justification
45: 10. **Constructive Suggestions** — Actionable improvements the authors can make

1: # ACL Conference Review Format
2: 
3: ## Conference
4: ACL (Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics) via ACL Rolling Review (ARR)
5: 
6: ## Review Criteria
7: Papers are evaluated on soundness, excitement/novelty, reproducibility, and societal impact. ACL uses the ARR review form with multiple scoring dimensions. Submissions go through ARR for review, then are committed to *ACL conferences for acceptance decisions.
8: 
9: ## Scoring Rubric
10: 
11: ### Overall Assessment (1–5, half-point increments)
12: | Score | Meaning |
13: |-------|---------|
14: | 5 | Consider for Award — outstanding paper (top 2.5%) |
15: | 4.5 | Borderline Award |
16: | 4 | Conference — could be accepted to an *ACL conference |
17: | 3.5 | Borderline Conference |
18: | 3 | Findings — could be accepted to Findings of the ACL |
19: | 2.5 | Borderline Findings |
20: | 2 | Resubmit next cycle — needs substantial revisions completable next cycle |
21: | 1.5 | Resubmit after next cycle — needs substantial revisions not completable next cycle |
22: | 1 | Do not resubmit — must be fully redone or not relevant to *ACL |
23: 
24: ### Soundness (1–5, half-point increments)
25: | Score | Meaning |
26: |-------|---------|
27: | 5 | Excellent — one of the most thorough studies given its type |
28: | 4 | Strong — sufficient support for all claims; extra experiments nice but not essential |
29: | 3 | Acceptable — sufficient support for main claims; minor points may need extra support |
30: | 2 | Poor — some main claims not sufficiently supported; major technical/methodological problems |
31: | 1 | Major Issues — not yet sufficiently thorough for publication |
32: 
33: ### Excitement (1–5, half-point increments)
34: | Score | Meaning |
35: |-------|---------|
36: | 5 | Highly Exciting — would recommend to others and attend its presentation |
37: | 4 | Exciting — would mention to others and make effort to attend |
38: | 3 | Interesting — might mention some points to others |
39: | 2 | Potentially Interesting — does not resonate with me but might with others |
40: | 1 | Not Exciting — does not resonate, and unlikely to resonate with others |
41: 
42: ### Reviewer Confidence (1–5)
43: | Score | Meaning |
44: |-------|---------|
45: | 5 | Positive that my evaluation is correct. Read very carefully, familiar with related work |
46: | 4 | Quite sure. Checked important points carefully. Unlikely I missed something |
47: | 3 | Pretty sure, but there's a chance I missed something. Good feel for the area but didn't check all details |
48: | 2 | Willing to defend evaluation, but fairly likely I missed some details or can't be sure about novelty |
49: | 1 | Not my area, or paper is very hard to understand. Evaluation is an educated guess |
50: 
51: ### Reproducibility (1–5)
52: | Score | Meaning |
53: |-------|---------|
54: | 5 | Could easily reproduce the results |
55: | 4 | Could mostly reproduce; some variation from sample variance or minor interpretation differences |
56: | 3 | Could reproduce with difficulty; parameters underspecified or data not widely available |
57: | 2 | Hard to reproduce; depends on unavailable data or insufficient detail |
58: | 1 | Could not reproduce no matter how hard they tried |
59: 
60: ### Datasets (1–5)
61: | Score | Meaning |
62: |-------|---------|
63: | 5 | Enabling — should affect others' choice of research projects |
64: | 4 | Useful — would recommend to others for ongoing work |
65: | 3 | Potentially useful — someone might find them useful |
66: | 2 | Documentary — useful to replicate reported research only |
67: | 1 | No usable datasets submitted |
68: 
69: ### Software (1–5)
70: | Score | Meaning |
71: |-------|---------|
72: | 5 | Enabling — should affect others' choice of research projects |
73: | 4 | Useful — would recommend to others for ongoing work |
74: | 3 | Potentially useful — someone might find it useful |
75: | 2 | Documentary — useful to replicate reported research only |
76: | 1 | No usable software released |
77: 
78: ## Required Sections
79: 
80: 1. **Paper Summary** — Describe what this paper is about (3-5 sentences). Help ACs/SACs understand the topic and highlight possible misunderstandings.
81: 2. **Summary of Strengths** — Major reasons to publish at a selective *ACL venue. Cite methodology, results, theory, organization.
82: 3. **Summary of Weaknesses** — Concerns about correctness, impact, clarity, or other issues. Number each concern for point-by-point response.
83: 4. **Questions for Authors** — Clarifying questions for the author response period.
84: 5. **Soundness** — Score (1–5) with justification. Is the paper sufficiently sound and thorough for its type?
85: 6. **Excitement** — Score (1–5) with justification. How exciting/novel/impactful is this work?
86: 7. **Overall Assessment** — Score (1–5) with justification. Should this paper be accepted at conference, Findings, or resubmitted?
87: 8. **Best Paper Justification** — Required only if Overall Assessment ≥ 4.5. Why this paper could be considered for an award.
88: 9. **Reviewer Confidence** — Score (1–5) with justification.
89: 10. **Limitations and Societal Impact** — Have the authors adequately discussed limitations and potential societal impacts? Suggest improvements if not.
90: 11. **Ethical Concerns** — Any ethical issues per ACL Code of Ethics? Extent to which they are acknowledged/addressed.
91: 12. **Needs Ethics Review** — Should this paper be sent for in-depth ethics review? Yes/No with explanation.
92: 13. **Reproducibility** — Score (1–5) with justification.
93: 14. **Datasets** — Score (1–5). How valuable are any released datasets?
94: 15. **Software** — Score (1–5). How valuable is any released software?
95: 16. **Comments/Suggestions/Typos** — Additional comments for authors beyond addressing weaknesses above.
96: 
97: ## Format Template
98: 
99: ```markdown

101:

103: **Reviewer**: Claude (AI Reviewer)
104:

<type>file</type>
<content>
1: #!/usr/bin/env python3
2: """Gemini Vision: figure and image analysis for academic papers.
3: 
4: Uses the Google Gemini API (google-genai SDK) for multimodal analysis.
5: Text understanding stays with Claude Code — this tool handles images only.
6: 
7: Subcommands:
8:   analyze     — Analyze one or more image files with a text prompt
9:   pdf-figures — Upload a PDF and analyze all figures within it
10: """
11: 
12: import argparse
13: import json
14: import os
15: import pathlib
16: import sys
17: import time
18: 
19: 
20: def _load_env():
21:     """Load .env file if python-dotenv is available."""
22:     try:
23:         from dotenv import load_dotenv
24:         load_dotenv()
25:     except ImportError:
26:         pass
27: 
28: 
29: def _get_client():
30:     """Create a Gemini client using the API key from environment."""
31:     _load_env()
32:     api_key = os.environ.get("GEMINI_API_KEY")
33:     if not api_key:
34:         print("Error: GEMINI_API_KEY not set. Add it to .env or export it.", file=sys.stderr)
35:         sys.exit(1)
36:     from google import genai
37:     return genai.Client(api_key=api_key)
38: 
39: 
40: def _mime_type(path: str) -> str:
41:     """Infer MIME type from file extension."""
42:     ext = pathlib.Path(path).suffix.lower()
43:     return {
44:         ".png": "image/png",
45:         ".jpg": "image/jpeg",
46:         ".jpeg": "image/jpeg",
47:         ".gif": "image/gif",
48:         ".webp": "image/webp",
49:         ".pdf": "application/pdf",
50:     }.get(ext, "image/png")
51: 
52: 
53: DEFAULT_FIGURE_PROMPT = """\
54: Analyze all figures, charts, and diagrams in this paper. For each figure:
55: 1. What is the figure number and caption?
56: 2. What does the figure show (axes, data series, visual elements)?
57: 3. What is the key takeaway or conclusion from this figure?
58: 4. Are there any notable patterns, trends, or anomalies?
59: 
60: Return the result as a JSON object with a "figures" array. Each entry should have:
61: - "figure_id": the figure number or identifier
62: - "caption": the figure caption text
63: - "description": detailed description of what the figure shows
64: - "key_takeaway": the main conclusion from this figure
65: """
66: 
67: DEFAULT_IMAGE_PROMPT = """\
68: Describe this figure from an academic paper in detail:
69: 1. What does the figure show (axes, data series, visual elements)?
70: 2. What is the key takeaway or conclusion?
71: 3. Are there any notable patterns, trends, or anomalies?
72: """
73: 
74: 
75: def analyze_images(image_paths: list[str], prompt: str, model: str) -> dict:
76:     """Analyze one or more image files with a text prompt."""
77:     from google.genai import types
78: 
79:     client = _get_client()
80:     contents = []
81: 
82:     for path in image_paths:
83:         p = pathlib.Path(path)
84:         if not p.exists():
85:             print(f"Error: file not found: {path}", file=sys.stderr)
86:             sys.exit(1)
87:         if p.stat().st_size > 20 * 1024 * 1024:
88:             print(f"Error: file too large (>20MB): {path}", file=sys.stderr)
89:             sys.exit(1)
90: 
91:         data = p.read_bytes()
92:         mime = _mime_type(path)
93:         contents.append(types.Part.from_bytes(data=data, mime_type=mime))
94: 
95:     contents.append(prompt)
96: 
97:     response = client.models.generate_content(
98:         model=model,
99:         contents=contents,
100:         config=types.GenerateContentConfig(temperature=0.2),
101:     )
102: 
103:     return {"prompt": prompt, "images": image_paths, "analysis": response.text}
104: 
105: 
106: def pdf_figures(pdf_path: str, prompt: str, model: str) -> dict:
107:     """Upload a PDF and analyze figures within it via the Gemini File API."""
108:     p = pathlib.Path(pdf_path)
109:     if not p.exists():
110:         print(f"Error: file not found: {pdf_path}", file=sys.stderr)
111:         sys.exit(1)
112:     if p.stat().st_size > 2 * 1024 * 1024 * 1024:
113:         print(f"Error: file too large (>2GB): {pdf_path}", file=sys.stderr)
114:         sys.exit(1)
115: 
116:     from google.genai import types
117: 
118:     client = _get_client()
119: 
120:     # Upload the PDF via the File API
121:     print(f"Uploading {pdf_path} ...", file=sys.stderr)
122:     uploaded_file = client.files.upload(file=str(p))
123:     print(f"Uploaded: {uploaded_file.name}", file=sys.stderr)
124: 
125:     # Wait for processing if needed
126:     max_wait = 120
127:     waited = 0
128:     while uploaded_file.state.name == "PROCESSING" and waited < max_wait:
129:         time.sleep(2)
130:         waited += 2
131:         uploaded_file = client.files.get(name=uploaded_file.name)
132: 
133:     if uploaded_file.state.name == "FAILED":
134:         print(f"Error: file processing failed", file=sys.stderr)
135:         client.files.delete(name=uploaded_file.name)
136:         sys.exit(1)
137: 
138:     if uploaded_file.state.name == "PROCESSING":
139:         print(f"Error: file processing timed out after {max_wait}s", file=sys.stderr)
140:         client.files.delete(name=uploaded_file.name)
141:         sys.exit(1)
142: 
143:     # Query the model about figures
144:     response = client.models.generate_content(
145:         model=model,
146:         contents=[uploaded_file, prompt],
147:         config=types.GenerateContentConfig(temperature=0.2),
148:     )
149: 
150:     result = {
151:         "pdf_path": pdf_path,
152:         "prompt": prompt,
153:         "analysis": response.text,
154:     }
155: 
156:     # Clean up the uploaded file
157:     try:
158:         client.files.delete(name=uploaded_file.name)
159:         print(f"Cleaned up: {uploaded_file.name}", file=sys.stderr)
160:     except Exception:
161:         pass
162: 
163:     return result
164: 
165: 
166: def main():
167:     parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
168:         description="Gemini Vision: figure/image analysis for academic papers"
169:     )
170:     sub = parser.add_subparsers(dest="command")
171: 
172:     # analyze subcommand
173:     a = sub.add_parser("analyze", help="Analyze image file(s) with a text prompt")
174:     a.add_argument("images", nargs="+", help="Path(s) to image file(s)")
175:     a.add_argument(
176:         "--prompt", "-p", default=DEFAULT_IMAGE_PROMPT,
177:         help="Analysis prompt (default: academic figure description)"
178:     )
179:     a.add_argument("--model", default="gemini-2.5-flash", help="Gemini model name")
180: 
181:     # pdf-figures subcommand
182:     pf = sub.add_parser("pdf-figures", help="Upload PDF and analyze all figures")
183:     pf.add_argument("pdf", help="Path to PDF file")
184:     pf.add_argument(
185:         "--prompt", "-p", default=DEFAULT_FIGURE_PROMPT,
186:         help="Analysis prompt (default: figure analysis)"
187:     )
188:     pf.add_argument("--model", default="gemini-2.5-flash", help="Gemini model name")
189: 
190:     args = parser.parse_args()
191: 
192:     if args.command == "analyze":
193:         result = analyze_images(args.images, args.prompt, args.model)
194:         print(json.dumps(result, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2))
195: 
196:     elif args.command == "pdf-figures":
197:         result = pdf_figures(args.pdf, args.prompt, args.model)
198:         print(json.dumps(result, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2))
199: 
200:     else:
201:         parser.print_help()
202: 
203: 
204: if __name__ == "__main__":
205:     main()

(End of file - total 205 lines)
